nativeRange.habitat: Found in inland freshwater lakes, ponds, slow-moving rivers, and streams, typically in areas with abundant aquatic vegetation and biofilm-covered surfaces for grazing.

diet.notes: Generally regarded as a grazing omnivore that subsists on biofilm, soft algae, and decaying plant matter. Has been documented accepting shrimp pellets, algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, cucumber), and small amounts of high-protein foods. Calcium-enriched or mineral-supplemented foods are often recommended to support successful molting.

goodWith: other dwarf shrimp species (when not interbreeding-compatible)
small peaceful nano fish (chili rasboras, ember tetras, pygmy corydoras)

summary: The Cherry Shrimp (Neocaridina davidi) is a small, peaceful freshwater invertebrate from East Asia, available in dozens of selectively bred color forms. It is generally considered one of the most beginner-friendly shrimp in the hobby and well suited to planted nano aquariums.
careNotes:
This entry covers the full Neocaridina davidi color-morph complex. All commercially available color forms — including Red Cherry, Sakura, Fire Red, Painted Fire Red, Bloody Mary, Yellow Neon, Yellow Sunkist, Green Jade, Blue Dream, Blue Velvet, Blue Diamond, Black Rose, Red Rili, Blue Rili, Carbon Rili, Snow White, Orange Pumpkin, and Chocolate — share identical care requirements. Importantly, these morphs will interbreed freely, and mixing them in the same tank tends to produce wild-type brown offspring over subsequent generations. The species is also sold under the older name Neocaridina heteropoda, which is still seen in some care guides; the two names refer to the same animal.

Cherry Shrimp are generally tolerant of a wide pH and hardness range but do best in stable, well-established aquariums where biofilm (the thin layer of microorganisms that colonizes surfaces in a mature tank) has had time to develop. Sudden swings in water parameters are commonly cited as a primary cause of die-offs, so acclimating new shrimp slowly and avoiding large, rapid water changes is widely advised.

The species is highly sensitive to copper, ammonia, and nitrite. Any medication or snail treatment containing copper should never be used in a tank housing shrimp, and Cherry Shrimp should only be added to a fully cycled aquarium — one where the nitrogen cycle is established and ammonia and nitrite consistently read zero.

Cherry Shrimp have been reported as invasive in thermally polluted waterways in Japan, the United States, Poland, and Germany. They should never be released into the wild.
breedingNotes: Generally considered one of the easiest aquarium invertebrates to breed — colonies will typically reproduce on their own in a stable, well-planted tank with no fish predators. Females become sexually mature at roughly 4–6 months and carry 20–30 eggs tucked under the abdomen (hobbyists describe a female in this state as 'berried', because the cluster of eggs resembles a bunch of tiny berries) for around 30 days. Newly hatched shrimplets are fully formed miniatures of the adults with no larval stage and feed immediately on biofilm. Raising the temperature toward the upper end of the tolerated range is commonly reported to accelerate breeding cycles. Mixed-morph colonies tend to revert toward wild-type brown coloration over several generations.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary care guide; source for temperature optimum (22-24°C), pH 6.5-8.5, gH ≥6, kH ≥2, group size ≥10, 10-gallon colony minimum.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on access; substituted with Fishkeeping World and Wikipedia. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Secondary substitute (Shrimp Farm 403'd). Cross-confirmed size ~3.8 cm, lifespan 1-2 years, pH 6.5-8.0, gH 4-14, temperature 23-29°C, copper sensitivity, beginner care level.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Neocaridina davidi)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback for family Atyidae / order Decapoda, synonymy with Neocaridina heteropoda, and confirmation of native range (China, Taiwan, Korea, Vietnam) and 3-4 cm adult size.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Cherry Shrimp generally molt roughly every 3–6 weeks as they grow, with reported intervals shortening for younger individuals and lengthening for mature adults. Discarded exoskeletons are commonly left in the tank and re-consumed by the shrimp for calcium; they should not be removed. Insufficient mineral content — low general hardness (gH) or low dietary calcium — is frequently associated with a condition hobbyists call the 'white ring of death', in which the shrimp fails to shed the old shell cleanly and dies trapped partway through the molt. Stable water parameters are widely considered the single most important factor in successful molting.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Breeds readily in any stable, predator-free planted tank. See top-level breedingNotes for details.
